coredata.dc:title: Graphene quantum dot-stabilized gold nanoparticles as a new colorimetric probe for in situ quantification of phenytoin in biological samples
coredata.dc:creator: Khoubnasabjafari, Maryam
Samadi, Azam
Jouyban, Abolghasem
coredata.dc:description: A rapid, accurate and precise spectrophotometric procedure was described for in situ quantification of phenytoin (PHT) using graphene quantum dots (GQD) stabilized on gold nanoparticles. It was achieved by synchronization of the GQD-gold nanoparticles synthesis and analyte determination via simple mixing PHT, as-prepared GQD obtained by direct pyrolysis of citric acid and HAuCl4 within 30 min at room temperature. The X-ray diffraction patterns and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy clearly confirmed the graphene honeycomb lattice of the graphene in nanoscale structure of gold nanoparticles. In addition, transmission electron microscopy, fluorescence, and ultraviolet–visible spectroscopies revealed the mechanism of aggregation of GQD supported gold nanoparticles for the detection of PHT. The proposed colorimetric assay is able to determine concentrations of PHT in the range of 0.01–0.20 µg·mL−1 with a low limit of detection (5 ng·mL−1) in human plasma obtained from patients taking PHT.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of (a) GQD stabilized AuNPs, (b) The size distribution histogram of GQD-AuNP and (c) GQD stabilized AuNPs in the presence of PHT (200 ng·mL−1).
FTIR spectra of GQD and GQD stabilized AuNPs.
XRD pattern of GQD and GQD stabilized AuNPs.

(a) Absorption spectra of in-situ prepared GQD stabilized AuNPs at different concentrations of PHT. (b) The calibration graph for in-situ determination of PHT using GQD stabilized AuNPs. Inset is the selected photograph of colorimetric response of GQD stabilized AuNPs for different concentrations of PHT 0 (b), 0.01(1), 0.025 (2), 0.05 (3), 0.1 (4), 0.15 (5) and 0.20 µg mL−1 (6).
Interference study of some drugs on the accuracy of assay results (PHT; 0.1 µg·mL−1).

row.entry: Tolerance limit* (interfering to analyte mass ratio)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Results of real sample analysis and accuracy test.

row.entry: Given method a Reference method t-value b

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Critical t-value at %95 confidence level are 4.30 and 19.00.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Comparison between the proposed assay and other spectrophotometric methods.

head.ce:title: Graphene quantum dot-stabilized gold nanoparticles as a new colorimetric probe for in situ quantification of phenytoin in biological samples

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Tuberculosis and Lung Diseases Research Center, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, Tabriz, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Tuberculosis and Lung Diseases Research Center

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Tuberculosis and Lung Diseases Research Center, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, Tabriz, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Pharmaceutical Analysis Research Center and Faculty of Pharmacy, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, Tabriz, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Pharmaceutical Analysis Research Center and Faculty of Pharmacy

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Pharmaceutical Analysis Research Center and Faculty of Pharmacy, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, Tabriz, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Digestive Diseases Research Institute, Tehran University of Medical Sciences, Tehran, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Digestive Diseases Research Institute, Tehran University of Medical Sciences, Tehran, Iran

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Pharmaceutical Analysis Research Center and Faculty of Pharmacy, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, Tabriz, Iran.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A rapid, accurate and precise spectrophotometric procedure was described for in situ quantification of phenytoin (PHT) using graphene quantum dots (GQD) stabilized on gold nanoparticles. It was achieved by synchronization of the GQD-gold nanoparticles synthesis and analyte determination via simple mixing PHT, as-prepared GQD obtained by direct pyrolysis of citric acid and HAuCl4 within 30 min at room temperature. The X-ray diffraction patterns and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy clearly confirmed the graphene honeycomb lattice of the graphene in nanoscale structure of gold nanoparticles. In addition, transmission electron microscopy, fluorescence, and ultraviolet–visible spectroscopies revealed the mechanism of aggregation of GQD supported gold nanoparticles for the detection of PHT. The proposed colorimetric assay is able to determine concentrations of PHT in the range of 0.01–0.20 µg·mL−1 with a low limit of detection (5 ng·mL−1) in human plasma obtained from patients taking PHT.

ce:section.ce:para: Phenytoin (PHT; 5,5-diphenylhydantoin) (Fig. S1; Electronic Supporting Material), an antiepileptic drug, is used to stop the propagation of seizures induced by cortical activity. It acts on neuronal sodium ion channels and lowers transmembrane Na+ flux, and so stabilizes neuronal cell membranes [1]. Therapeutic drug monitoring of PHT is necessary to secure its effectiveness and riskless levels, because some factors such as narrow therapeutic window (10–20 μg·mL−1), high degree of protein binding, and nonlinear pharmacokinetics complicate PHT dosing [2].

The common analytical techniques that are applied for PHT quantification include gas chromatography [3], liquid chromatography [4–7], micellar electrokinetic chromatography [8], immunoassay technique [9], and spectrophotometry [10–15].

In the last decades, metal nanoparticles (NP), especially gold nanoparticles (AuNPs), have gained significant attraction in different research areas due to their unique surface, high electron density, and strong optical absorption. The promising application of AuNPs is their use as colorimetric sensors due to a small alteration on their surface which causes a great change in their color as well as in their localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) band [16–19]. Graphene quantum dots (GQD) are another important nanomaterial that has superior properties such as low toxicity, excellent biocompatibility, photoluminescence, and electron transfer behavior. Their two latter properties have largely been used for the sensing of a variety of analytes, including metal ions, anions and small molecules, and drugs [20–22].

Recently, the green synthesis of GQD-stabilized metal NP that is a consequence of the electron transfer behavior of GQD have attracted great attention [23–25]. In this work, taking the GQD as an electron donor and HAuCl4 as an electron acceptor into account, AuNPs were synthesized successfully at room temperature by simply mixing GQD and HAuCl4. It was found that upon adding PHT, the optical properties of in-situ generated AuNPs changed and made possible the quantitative assay of PHT in plasma samples. There are a few reports for the analytical application of GQD-stabilized gold nanoparticles, especially colorimetric ones [26]. In addition, the present method is the first report of the simultaneous determination of analytes and synthesis of GQD-stabilized AuNPs (in just one step). It is noteworthy that synchronization of the in-situ synthesis and in-situ determination shortened the time of analysis significantly. Moreover, the determination of PHT using the LSPR band of metal NP is still rare. Our previous report of the in-situ determination of phenytoin using branched gold nanoparticles included the use of poisonous methanol and high temperature as reducing agents that are somewhat undesirable [11]. However, this work presents a green procedure for the determination of phenytoin without the need for complicated accessories.

All reagents used were of analytical grade and purchased from Merck (Darmstadt, Germany). PHT sodium was a gift from Alhavi Pharmaceutical Company (Tehran, Iran). Carbamazepine, sodium valproate, phenobarbital, and levetiracetam were provided by other Iranian pharmaceutical companies (Arastoo, Temad, Amin, and Aburaihan, respectively). An LC apparatus (Knauer, Germany) with a Nova-Pak@ C18 analytical column and UV detector (λ = 220 nm) were used for HPLC analyses. The mobile phase consisted of a mixture of potassium dihydrogen phosphate buffer (pH 6.0)-acetonitrile-2-propanol (63:22:15, v/v/v) at a constant flow rate of 1.0 mL·min−1 [27].

Absorbance spectra were measured on Shimadzu UV-1800 UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Kyoto, Japan). A Jasco FP-750 spectrofluorometer (Kyoto, Japan) was used to provide fluorescence spectra with a micro-volume cell with 1.0 cm path length. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Philips CM120, operated at 430 kV) was used for the characterization of NP. Fourier transforms infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy measurements were acquired by Bruker, Tensor 27 (USA). A Siemens D500 X-ray diffractometer with a Cu target (λ = 0.1540 nm) was used to provide the XRD pattern of samples.

The pyrolysis of citric acid was used to prepare GQD according to previously reported work [28]. GQD showed a well-defined absorption band at 357 nm, Fig S2a, with the maximum excitation wavelength and the maximum emission wavelength at 365 and 475 nm, respectively (Fig S2b). TEM image of GQD as shown in Fig. S2c, indicated the preparation of well dispersed nanoparticles with anaverage diameterof 8 nm.

Proper amounts of PHT, GQD, BR buffer (pH = 9.0, 0.12 mol·L−1), NaCl, and HAuCl4 were added one by one in order into a 2-mL microtube. Then, the solutions were diluted to 800 µL and gently mixed. The absorbance values at 523 and 605 nm were measured after incubation at room temperature for 30 min. In all measurements, deionized water was used as the blank.

Iranian Blood Transfusion Organization (Tabriz, Iran) provided drug-free plasma samples. Under the approval of the Ethics Committee of Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, a consent letter (IR.TBZMED.REC.1398.670) was signed by all plasma sample donors. The plasma samples frozen at −20 °C were thawed to room temperature before analysis. Dispersive liquid–liquid microextraction according to a previously reported method was used to prepare 100 μL of plasma samples [29]. Then, evaporation of sedimentation phase resulting from the extraction step was done using a gentle stream of nitrogen under a hood at room temperature. Then, 200 µL of reconstituted sample was used to determine PHT based on the general procedure.

According to previously published works, GQD were synthesized using citric acid pyrolysis [28] and applied to the synthesis of AuNPs [24,30,31]. The orange color of GQD showed an absorption band around 350 nm, whereas adding HAuCl4·3H2O provoked an instant formation of the red AuNPs color (Fig. S3) related to the reduction of the Au (III) into the Au (0) and the formation of AuNPs with a surface plasmon absorption band around 530 nm. The TEM image of this solution (Fig. 1 a) also confirmed the presence of spherical AuNPs with a pretty narrow distribution of 10 ± 4 nm (Fig. 1b).

In FTIR spectra of GQD (Fig. 2 ), the strong bands at 3419 and 1579 cm−1 can be assigned to the stretching vibration O–H and the aromatic C = C bonds, respectively. In addition, the peaks at 2928, 2854, and 1395 cm−1 are of the stretching vibration and bending vibration mode of C–H, respectively. Interestingly, all the skeletal and stretching vibrations of the aromatic rings can also be observed in the FTIR spectra of GQD-stabilized AuNPs, confirming the honeycomb lattice of the graphene structure in the AuNPs.

The XRD pattern of GQD (Fig. 3 ) exhibited only a broad diffraction peak at 2θ≈30° that can be indexed to the graphite (002) plane. However, there are several diffraction peaks in the XRD pattern of AuNPs-stabilized GQD that can be assigned to the nanogold structure. The diffraction peaks at 2θ degrees of 31.84, 38.22, 45.59, 56.82, and 62.22 assigned to planes (012), (301), (013), (204), and (232) confirm the crystalline nature of AuNPs in the AuNPs-stabilized GQD structure [32].

Interestingly, in the presence of PHT molecules during the formation of AuNPs, the color changed from red to blue, appropriate with increasing PHT concentration (Fig. 4 ), and TEM images confirm that this occurred because of AuNPs aggregation (Fig. 1c). In addition, experimental evidence suggests that aggregation occurred by the binding of PHT molecules to the nanoparticle surface via hydrogen bonding through the hydroxyl and carbonyl group of GQD-stabilized AuNPs [33]. To confirm the aggregation mechanism, GQD-stabilized AuNPs were prepared using the general procedure of blank solution. Then, the ex-situ determination of different concentrations of PHT was tested using 500 μL of GQD-stabilized AuNPs. The results showed that the PHT molecules could aggregate GQD-stabilized AuNPs using the ex-situ procedure, too; however, a longer time (~2h) was needed compared to the in-situ aggregation strategy. Thus, it was assumed that the aggregation of AuNPs before the complete stabilization of AuNPs is responsible for the quick in-situ colorimetric assay compared to the ex-situ one.

The fluorescence property of GQD was also studied to further investigate the mechanism of the present assay (Fig. S4). The fluorescence of GQD was turned off by AuNPs through the formation of GQD-stabilized AuNPs due to the fluorescence resonance energy transfer. Fluorescence quenching is proportional to the concentration of HAuCl4 in the 0.025 to 0.25 mmol L−1 range (data not shown); however, the fluorescence intensity of GQD was not recovered by adding PHT during the formation of AuNPs. This evidence confirms that AuNPs and GQD are in a bonded state, and PHT molecules adsorb on GQD-stabilized AuNPs. Thus, the fluorescence intensity did not change remarkably, and it was not used as a measurement parameter for determining PHT.

To set up the optimum conditions for PHT assay, the parameters affecting measurement signal, including (a) amount of GQD, (b) sample pH and amount of buffer, (c) HAuCl4 concentration, (d) NaCl concentration, and (e) incubation time, were investigated (Fig. S5). The optimum amounts of effective parameters are as follows: (a) GQD: 150 µL; (b) pH: 9.0 (100 µL of BR buffer was used for adjustment pH); (c) HAuCl4: 0.075 mmol·L−1; (d) NaCl: 12.5 mmol·L−1; (e) incubation time: 30 min.

The interference limits of some most commonly co-administered drugs on the measurement parameters were investigated (Table 1 ). A relative error of < 5% in the presence of the added species was taken as the tolerance limit. The results showed that sodium valproate, phenobarbital, carbamazepine, and levetiracetam did not interfere with the PHT quantification.

Under optimum conditions, (A523/A605)0/(A523/A605) (A◦ and A are absorbance intensity values related to blank and standard solution, respectively) was plotted versus PHT concentration. (A523/A605)0/(A523/A605) was deliberately considered as a measurement parameter, because the changes in SPR spectra at different concentrations did not agree with the common aggregation based assays. These differences result from the in-situ aggregation mechanism and the coincidence of synthesis and aggregating processes. In Fig. 4, the absorbance of the GQD-stabilized AuNPs increased for the initial four additions, but a small shift in SPR wavelength toward a longer wavelength when the SPR band peak was broadened was also observed. It is known that increasing the particle size red-shifts the SPR wavelength and also increases the intensity [34,35]. Thus, it was assumed that large AuNPs are formed in the presence of low concentrations of PHT molecules. At higher concentrations of PHT, however, a new peak at longer wavelength with decreases in the SPR band peak was observed. It was inferred that at high concentrations, PHT molecules could aggregate the GQD-stabilized AuNPs nanoparticles by forming hydrogen bonds.

The shifts in SPR bands for first three/four additions are very meager. However, the overall differences of SPR peaks at different concentrations are very obvious. Thus, to achieve the effective and accurate results, the ratiometric measurement was chosen for the determination of phenytoin using the present probe. Ratiometric measurements detect in a single sample two parameters that are analyzed by calculating their ratio. The SPR band intensity at 523 and 605 nm were used to calculate the ratiometric parameter. The 605 nm was used to track the changes in the longer wavelength since it gave a wider linear range with a high correlation coefficient than to other wavelengths e.g. 630 nm. The data of calibration plot was obtained by dividing the absorbance intensity ratio at 523 and 605 nm of blank (A523/A605)0 into (A523/A605) at each concentration.

There was a good linear relationship between the measurement parameter and the PHT concentration in the range of 0.01–0.20 µg·mL−1 with a detection limit (LOD) of 0.005 µg·mL−1 (LOD = 3×(standard deviation of blank)/slope of calibration plot) [36]. The present probe did not show any significant changes in wavelength shift or absorbance intensity at concentrations higher than 0.20 µg·mL−1 of PHT. The error bar was also added on calibration plot points to confirm the reliability of the obtained data. The corresponding correlation coefficient (r) was 0.9977 with equation ΔA = 9.052C (µg·mL−1) + 0.798 (Fig. 4). The relative standard deviation (RSD) for five replicated measurements determinations was 1.4% for 0.1 µg·mL−1 of PHT.

Three patient plasma samples were analyzed using the newly developed assay. It is noteworthy that the interference of biomolecules present in the real samples was eliminated by dispersive liquid–liquid microextraction as described in Section 2.5. For an accurate evaluation of the results, the findings were compared with those provided by using the standard HPLC method (Table 2 ). The PHT plasma concentrations in the real samples were below the therapeutic range, which is considered to be due to the effects of phenobarbital co-administration [37,38].

In summary, the as-prepared GQD obtained by direct pyrolysis of citric acid was applied as a reducing and stabilizing agent for AuNP production by adding extra reductants. UV–Vis spectroscopy, TEM, XRD, and FTIR characterizations clearly confirmed the formation of GQD-stabilized AuNPs that were used through a simple and quick in-situ strategy for the sensitive spectrophotometric assay of PHT. The preparation of GQD-stabilized AuNPs in the presence of PHT caused a shift to large and aggregated NPs with a color change from red to blue and an obvious red shift of the surface plasmon absorbance of AuNPs at high concentrations.

This in-situ assay presents some advantages such as speed, low cost, and no need for advanced analytical instruments. In comparison with spectrophotometric techniques, the newly developed colorimetric assay is also more sensitive than other ones (Table 3 ).

Maryam Khoubnasabjafari: Resources. Azam Samadi: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing - original draft. Abolghasem Jouyban: Writing - review & editing, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Research Affairs, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, under grant number 64109.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2020.105331.

The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
